data.title: Hinge Health: I Have A Positive Hinge On This Offering
data.description:
Shares of Hinge Health (NYSE:HNGE) made a strong public debut. Not only did the shares price at the high end of the preliminary offering range, shares rose nearly 20% coming out of the gates, as investors like this use of technology in healthcare.

Strong growth, in fact accelerating growth, and profitable operations make me upbeat as well amidst a reasonable sales multiple at first sight. This makes me more excited to learn about the business going forward, with an intention to get involved if more becomes known.

A New Health System

Founded back in 2012, Hinge Health has a mission to build a new health system which looks to transform outcomes, experience and costs with the aid of technology to both scale and automate care.

The company aims to do this with software (of course including AI) to automate care for joint and muscle health, resulting in improved experiences, improved member outcomes and cost reductions. With software focusing on motion tracking, there is an electrical nerve stimulation wearable device involved as well, to create optimal exercises in an automated manner.

Applications include a wide spectrum of so-called MSK care, ranging from acute injury, to chronic pain and post-surgical rehabilitation.

MKS is a very prevalent group of conditions, with some 40% of the US population estimated to suffer from such an indication. Many of these require physical therapy, but many patients never get this because of difficulty to access, high costs and often end up having expensive and risky procedures performed instead.

The company furthermore believes that it is successful in introducing technology in health. The company recognized that it is not new in trying to do this, yet predecessors suffered from the fact that healthcare intervention includes many unstructured physical tasks, while the current healthcare reimbursement system has specific pathways.

By focusing specifically on physical therapy markets, the company can save over 90% on therapy hours, with innovative payment options provided, including direct sales to employers.

By the end of 2024, the company had over half a million members, from over 2,000 clients. The company generated some $390 million in revenues that year, which implies an average of just over $700 in revenues per member.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Hinge Health initially aimed to sell nearly 14 million shares in a $28-$32 range, with final pricing set at the higher end of the range. With some 8.5 million shares sold by the business, gross process from the offering are pegged at $272 million.

With a total of some 80 million shares outstanding (in various classes) the company is granted a $2.2 billion equity valuation at the offer price. Pro forma cash holdings come in near $400 million, although that much of this position is netted out, taking into account liabilities and preferred stock, as I work with an enterprise valuation assumption just north of $2 billion here.

The company generated revenues of nearly $293 million in 2023, on which operating losses of $130 million were reported (GAAP accounting). Revenues rose by a solid 33% to $391 million, with revenues up nearly a hundred million in dollar terms. The same degree of leverage was achieved on the bottom line, with operating loss narrowing to $32 million, making that real progress was made here.

Looking at the quarterly cadence, we see revenues (rounded at a midpoint) at $90 million, $101 million, $117 million and $124 million over the past fourth quarters, with the last quarter ending in March of this year. Moreover, the company posted operating profits in the two most recent quarter, posted at a sizable near $14 million number in the most recent quarter.

With revenues trending at half a billion and operating profits around $60 million, valuations are demanding, but the profitability and growth is very comforting. With a flattish balance sheet, a $60 million operating income number could yield after tax earnings around $50 million, for earnings around $0.60 per share. While an offering priced at 50 times earnings is demanding, it comes with impressive growth, with revenue growth accelerating a long way to the 50% mark in the most recent quarter.

For these reasons, shares rose to the $38 mark on the first day of trading. This pushed up the market value of the firm by roughly half a billion and based on a roughly $2.7 billion enterprise valuation, I see the business valued at around 5.5 times sales, and a 60 time earnings multiple.

These are high multiples, but the technology, business model and growth is impressive, which not automatically make me dismiss these valuation concerns here.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks for the company and this offering, like any offering or investment opportunity, are plentiful.

First and foremost is the valuation as quite some growth is priced into the stock, with this growth itself relying on high retention rates, reliance on relations with a few big PBMs, pricing for the services which it receives, and various classes of stock outstanding. One major source of concern is competition of course, as more players like Kaia Health, Omada Health, Sword Health and Vori health are active in similar markets.

Frankly, I am quite upbeat on the business, but not willing to chase at current levels, based on the current information. I look forward to the first quarterly results released as a publicly traded business to better understand the growth cadence and margin developments. For now, I have an upbeat undertone, but need to get more comfortable with the strength of the solutions and business model before committing capital to work.

data.title: XPeng Is Crushing It
data.description:
XPeng (XPEV) (OTCPK:XPNGF) reported better-than-expected results for its first-quarter due to growing momentum for the company’s deliveries. XPeng has trailed its rivals in the electric vehicle market in terms of both deliveries and vehicle margins in the last several years, but the EV maker is making great progress, especially with regard to deliveries. In the first-quarter, XPeng’s deliveries surged 331% year-over-year, due in part to a rapid upsurge of Mona-branded EVs, and the company saw a sequential improvement in its vehicle margins as well. While I like XPeng's progress so far this year, I am still cautious with respect to the EV company's valuation.

Previous rating

I rated shares of XPeng a hold in my last work (and before that) -- Growth And Margin Upside -- due to shares of the EV maker being highly valued relative to other electric vehicle companies in the market. Further, XPeng was lagging its EV rivals significantly in terms of vehicle margins. XPeng’s deliveries have surged lately, however, chiefly because of a positive market reaction for XPeng’s low-cost EV model Mona 03. I believe the current trajectory has improved greatly, but with XPeng still having inferior margins and a high valuation, I maintain my hold rating.

XPeng beats earnings

XPeng beat top and bottom-line predictions for its first fiscal quarter on Wednesday: the electric vehicle enterprise reported adjusted earnings of $(0.06) per-share, beating Wall Street’s consensus estimate by $0.15 per-share. Revenues came in at $2.2B, out-matching the average prediction by $17.4M.

Vehicle margin trend is improving

XPeng has lagged its rivals in terms of vehicle margins for a while and the company still has the lowest margins in the industry group. However, XPeng’s margins are improving and have surged in the last year. As of the end of the March quarter, XPeng posted a vehicle margin of 10.5%, showing growth of 0.5 PP quarter-over-quarter. In the last twelve months, XPeng’s margins have grown by a full 5.0 PP.

Li Auto (LI), a top contender in the Chinese electric vehicle start-up market, had a vehicle margin of 19.7% in the fourth-quarter of FY 2024 (results for Q1’25 have not yet been reported). NIO (NIO), another serious EV enterprise in China, had a fourth-quarter vehicle margin of 13.1% (results for Q1’25 are only due in the first week of June). While XPeng is still behind its rivals in terms of vehicle margins, the EV firm is making steady progress here which I believe works to the benefit of XPeng.

In the first-quarter, XPeng delivered 94,008 electric vehicles, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 331%. At no time in the EV maker's history did the company deliver more vehicles than in Q1’25. In April, the EV company delivered a massive 35,045 electric vehicles -- according to its monthly delivery report -- showing 273% year-over-year growth and XPeng delivered more than 30,000 units in each of the last six months.

This growth is partly fueled by last year’s release of the Mona M03 which has seen steady delivery growth since its launch. In April 2025, 14,210 vehicles were Mona M03 models, representing a massive 41% total delivery share.

XPeng is going to try to repeat this success when later this month it will launch the competitively-priced Mona M03 Max in China. The Mona M03 Max is a coupe sedan with an initial price sticker of 150,000 Chinese Yuan ($20,805) and is targeting specifically younger buyers below the age of 35. The Mona M03 Max will be equipped with XPeng’s Turing AI Driving Assistance System and allegedly has a range of 580 km on a single charge.

The Mona M03 is a low-cost EV model that appeals to buyers in China that are more price conscious, with an initial starting price of 119,800 Chinese Yuan (approximately $16,815). The Mona M03 was launched in late August 2024 and is meant to serve a market segment of younger and more budget-conscious buyers.

XPeng’s vehicle-related revenue surged 159% year-over-year to 14.37B Chinese Yuan ($1.98B) in Q1'25, mainly because of the success of the Mona M03 and a massively growing delivery share of this model. With sales and deliveries ramping up in the triple-digits in the first-quarter, XPeng also benefited from a margin boost: its gross margin reached an all-time high at 15.6%, showing a 1.2 PP quarter-over-quarter gain.

XPeng’s valuation

XPeng’s shares are still expensive, relative to its rivals in the Chinese EV start-up industry group. Shares of XPeng are currently valued at a forward (FY 2026) price-to-revenue ratio of 1.4X which makes the company about 43% more expensive than Li Auto and 158% more expensive than NIO. The industry average group P/S ratio is ~1.0X.

NIO has the lowest price-to-revenue ratio of 0.53X and is widely underpriced, in my opinion. In fact, EV companies that I like the most include BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) due to its massive export momentum and cheap valuation -- A Better Bet Than Tesla -- and NIO which is just massively undervalued given its opportunity for ONVO-related growth. ONVO is NIO's low-cost brand with which it tries to lure new, younger buyers to its EV business and I believe NIO has a decent chance of doubling its valuation going forward if it can maintain its current growth momentum. I also like Li Auto due to the fact that the EV maker is leading the industry group in terms of vehicle margins: Time To Be Greedy.

XPeng, on the other hand, is already quite expensive, relative to other EV companies, all of which have higher vehicle margins than the Mona-producer. Since Li Auto is leading the start-up group in terms of margins, I would choose Li Auto for profitability reasons. NIO is by far the cheapest EV play, so I see the biggest rebound potential with this EV maker.

Risks with XPeng

The biggest risk for XPeng is a potential slowdown in Mona-related deliveries which are currently boosting the company’s total delivery prospects. A weak market reception of the Mona M03 Max version later in May could also be a potential cause for concern for investors. The vehicle and gross margin trajectory, however, is looking encouraging. What would change my mind about XPeng is if the company were to see a massive slowdown in Mona deliveries and a weakening vehicle margin trend.

Final thoughts

XPeng reported better-than-expected first-quarter earnings on Wednesday that highlighted the considerable progress the company has made, especially in terms of ramping up its (Mona) deliveries and improving its gross and vehicle margin profile. While I like the EV maker's progress, XPeng’s shares are much more expensive than those of its direct EV rivals in the Chinese start-up group, which is why I remain cautious about the company. I like XPeng’s Mona M03 ramp and believe that the Max version could also be a success, potentially accelerating the company’s delivery and revenue growth. Shares have surged more than 153% in the last year and since they are now much more expensive than those of Li Auto or NIO, I maintain my hold rating here.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Not The Dip I Want To Buy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS), the stock has risen +35%, compared to around -2% for the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same period. That's quite an alpha, and not unjustified. Since then, the company has delivered good results and a good evolution in the thesis, ranging from the concrete delivery of good numbers to relevant partnerships with Novo Nordisk (NVO).

HIMS stock fell 5% on Wednesday and it was down another 7.7% on Thursday due to Cigna's (CI) deal with Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly (LLY). Unlike the Q4 dip mentioned in my first article, this new dip is a move where I don't think it's a great opportunity.

This Deal Isn’t Good News For Hims & Hers

A few weeks ago, Hims & Hers announced its collaboration with Novo Nordisk. In the company's own words, this is a “long-term collaboration”, and aims to offer more affordable obesity care and treatments. This is a huge step forward for the thesis, which previously seemed to be a threat to the company and show that the platform was not a moat, now shows that even very consolidated pharmaceuticals want to participate together with Hims & Hers.

Now, the narrative has changed somewhat. Cigna will now offer weight-loss drugs like Wegovy and Zepbound for $200 a month, which is cheaper than Hims & Hers' offer, and much cheaper than Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly's price.

On the positive side, it's still possible to argue that GLP-1 drugs don't sum up Hims & Hers' thesis. The company is much more than that, and continues to execute its strategy well, adding more products, with strong growth in subscribers, revenue (as the chart below shows) and this converting into cash flow.

But it's undeniable that this Cigna deal reveals some of the risks of the thesis. It is a direct competitive risk, since Hims offers branded and compounded drugs. There is a decrease in the use of alternatives offered by Hims & Hers with the cost of the drugs at $200/m through Cigna, i.e. it eliminates one of the differentials that was price, practicality and the like. There is also a derivative of this risk, since with more competition, the company may gain fewer subscribers who would come because of this solution.

But in my opinion, more than the actual risk, this shows that the scenario is not static, quite the opposite. This market is constantly evolving and is very dynamic, with different partnerships and players emerging all the time, as well as changes in regulation and players adapting. This needs to be mentioned and needs to be considered in the analysis, there is a relatively large level of uncertainty, even though Hims & Hers is still doing a good job.

The Positive Points of the Thesis Continue

The positive points of the thesis continue. The company still has strong potential in its hands: to increase what it offers on the platform, to continue with good services, to bring in more customers and to increase revenue per customer. It's similar to theses like that of Nu Holdings (NU), whose core is to grow ARPAC, grow customers, but keep the cost to serve low. In the case of Hims & Hers, scale will also be very important and should continue to help generate cash.

The table below summarizes the company's strategy well. First it needs to acquire new customers, and it does this via ambassadors, partnerships, advertising (as seen at the Super Bowl); then it needs to retain these customers, and this is done through user experience and the like. If the customer has come to Hims for an obesity treatment, for example, if they see that it really is a company that offers a good, practical and cheap service, they may also opt for hair or various other treatments (which will still be expanded) that Hims & Hers offers. Finally, engagement increases revenue per subscriber.

Even with the ups and downs of GLP-1, Hims & Hers has not only created a great platform, but above all has the potential to continue growing and consolidating.

But HIMS' Valuation Has Worsened

Okay, the positive points of the thesis remain, but we must remember the uncertainty that comes with this market and this business model highlighted in the first section. The third relevant point, and the one that will be the “icing on the cake” to justify my downgrade, is valuation.

In Q4, annualized revenue per subscriber was $671. In Q1, this metric went up to $970, with the company's new offerings. Now, it doesn't seem so far-fetched to estimate that by 2027 this revenue per subscriber could be just over $1,000 a year. If it advances by around 4% a year (and maintains this strong base), it will be enough to reach something close to $1,100. This seems a reasonable assumption to me, since it takes into account a strong base and the ability to upsell and cross-sell, but it's also not overly optimistic due to the dynamism of the market.

As for the number of subscribers, I rounded the assumption up to 4 million. To achieve this by 2027, Hims & Hers will have to add around 600,000 customers a year, which also seems reasonable, and even conservative, given that they are currently adding something close to 200,000 per quarter.

Finally, I kept the 13.5% net margin assumption. With these adjustments, we now have an estimated net income of $594 million in 2027. This net income implies a price-to-earnings ratio of 20x.

Well, for a company with a lot of potential, 20x is not expensive. But note that one of the main changes in this estimate was the market cap, which even after these falls is $12 billion.

There is still the potential for Hims & Hers to continue to surprise, perhaps 4 million subscribers is just the beginning. Its total addressable market is gigantic, so it's not crazy to imagine that in a few decades this number could be several million. If that happens, the revenue will certainly be higher, the net margin will be higher, and consequently, the shareholder value generated will be much higher. In other words, in a still bullish scenario, Hims & Hers is still fairly priced.

However, there's no denying that the increase in the price of the stock in recent months, together with the dynamics of the market, reduced the margin of safety.

The Bottom Line

In view of the above information, although I still believe in the potential of Hims & Hers, I prefer to opt for a more neutral position at the moment. The current situation of greater uncertainty and an already relatively high market cap, reduces the margin of safety and consequently causes me to downgrade the rating.

Note that the “hold” here is not in an overly pessimistic tone, but because I believe that this environment is not enough for an optimistic “buy” rating, I opted for the downgrade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML: The Market's Miscalculation
data.description:
Investment thesis

The last time I have been covering ASML Holding N.V. stock (NASDAQ:ASML) was in March 2023. It was a "Buy" rating, and I have mixed feelings about the way it aged. On the one hand, the stock delivered a decent 16% return to investors over the period. On the other hand, this performance was far weaker compared to a 47% gain from the S&P 500 index.

ASML's fundamentals are robust, the valuation looks good, and AI tailwinds are strong. However, with such a relatively weak performance compared to the broader market over more than two years means that it would be unfair to upgrade ASML to the highest possible rating. Therefore, I reiterate a "Buy" rating for ASML.

Recent developments

The company released its latest quarterly earnings on April 16, demonstrating a double beat against consensus estimates. Revenue grew by staggering 56% YoY, which is a massive acceleration compared to a 22% YoY growth in the previous quarter.

The most important part for me as value investor, the bottom line, significantly outpaced revenue growth. The company's adjusted EPS more than doubled, from $3.32 to $6.83. Such a massive EPS growth is a notable bullish sign, especially considering the fact that the outstanding share count has been stable over the last several quarters.

The Q1 operating margin demonstrated solid expansion YoY, from 26.3% to 35.4%. This is a massive improvement for the company of ASML's scale which underscores both the management's commitment to operational efficiency and the company's robust pricing power. As a result, the free cash flow generating capacity is improving and ASML is highly likely to further improve its already fortress-like balance sheet.

The upcoming earnings release is scheduled for July 18, with the Q2 revenue expected by consensus to be $8.51 billion. This will be a massive 24.7% YoY revenue growth. The crucial fact is that the adjusted EPS is expected to further outpace the topline growth with the expected 34.6% YoY increase.

Such a bullish forecast from Wall Street analysts is fair because the AI revolution goes on and the prominent chip companies are in fierce technological battle. As a result, large semiconductor manufacturers are aggressively ramping up their manufacturing capacities.

The world's largest chip foundry, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) is expanding its production capacity extremely aggressively. The company's total investment in its U.S.-based facilities only is expected to reach $100 billion over the next few years. Nvidia has been outsourcing chip manufacturing to TSM for ages but now this company also plans to invest staggering amounts in its own manufacturing facilities. Nvidia reportedly plans to invest half-a-trillion in building out manufacturing capacity in the U.S. in partnership with TSM, Foxconn, and Amkor.

Apart from these giants, there are smaller players that plan to expand their chip manufacturing capacity in the near future. For example, Global Wafers company plans to boost its capex to $7.5 billion because chip demand is soaring. All these aggressive capital spending plans are extremely bullish for ASML as the company dominates the global EUV lithography systems market.

Relentless commitment to innovation appears to be the only recipe to protect technological edge, and ASML's management clearly understands rules of the game as the company's R&D spending continues soaring. With ASML's fortress balance sheet and massive FCF generating capacity, the company has great potential to remain at the forefront of innovation. It is crucial to emphasize that the management also does not forget to keep shareholders happy with generous buybacks, which underscores a balanced capital allocation approach.

Valuation update

ASML's valuation ratios look really good as current multiples are by far lower than historical averages across the board. I am not comparing to the sector median because of ASML's absolutely unique dominant positioning in the EUV lithography industry.

Reliance on debt is extremely low, meaning that cost of equity is the proper choice to discount the financial model. As usual, the CAPM approach is used to figure out the cost of equity, which is 9.49% in ASML's case.

The stock-based compensation [SBC] is quite low as well. Therefore, using the levered TTM margin without any adjustments is also prudent enough. This metric is at 25.5% at the moment, and the company's stellar record of the FCF margin expansion means that incorporating a one percentage point yearly growth is not overly optimistic. The FY2026 revenue assumption is $39.46 billion. For the base-case scenario, a 10% revenue CAGR is incorporated coupled with a 3% perpetual growth rate.

The base case scenario suggests that ASML is 43% undervalued because the company's fair value is close to $418 billion. The margin of safety is huge because ASML remains undervalued even if a super-conservative 5% revenue CAGR is applied.

As usual, in the final table I summarize the target price scenarios depending on the abovementioned revenue growth assumptions. Due to ASML's robust fundamentals and massive capex spending spree in the industry, I believe that a 10% CAGR is more likely. Therefore, my target price for ASML is around $1,054.

Risks update

The company's performance is susceptible to cycles in the broader semiconductor industry, which has a highly cyclical nature. Due to AI tailwinds, we are currently in a semiconductor super-cycle, but its longevity is highly uncertain. In addition, the industry is subject to technological disruptions, which is also a significant risk for ASML.

The stock's average performance over the last two decades has been quite volatile during summer months. June and August have relatively low win rates, meaning that there might be temporary pullbacks during these months. On the other hand, July's win rate is notably higher [which might be explained by Q2 earnings release as a historically robust catalyst].

Last but not least, the stock has been underperforming compared to the broader U.S. equity market over the last year. This might indicate that the market is not very impressed enough by ASML's robust fundamentals and attractive valuation. With such cautious optimism it might be difficult to expect a massive rally.

Bottom line

To conclude, ASML remains a "Buy" despite its fortress-like fundamentals and AI tailwinds. The reason behind not giving the stock the highest possible rating is because of its relatively weak performance compared to the broader U.S. equity market which might indicate historically weak sentiment around the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BlackRock Is Better Than NVIDIA For AI Infrastructure Investment
data.description:
During President Trump’s recent tour of the Middle East countries, investors' eyes were mostly glued to NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) and its partnership with Saudi Arabia’s new AI investment arm called HUMAIN to sell its most sophisticated Blackwell (GB200) chips.

This represents a breakthrough and has resulted in its shares gaining around 15%, as charted below.

Still, competitor Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and hyperscalers, which produce customized AI chips, have also secured deals as detailed later. Also, in the absence of a definite timeline for projects despite the big announcements, this thesis aims to show that the market may have become over-enthusiastic. Another important factor to consider is that when accelerated compute chips are purchased, they must be housed in data centers that need to be energized first.

In such a context, this thesis aims to show that it is better to get exposure to AI infrastructure through BlackRock (NYSE:BLK), whose CEO, Mr. Fink, was also present alongside big tech leaders in Riyadh. I start by differentiating the asset manager's and NVIDIA’s respective roles in the AI ecosystem.

Highlighting BlackRock's Financier Role

After GPUs (graphics processing units) are purchased from NVIDIA to train AI models, they need to be installed in data centers in the optimum environmental conditions, as these power-hungry systems require considerable energy and advanced cooling systems. These form part of the AI infrastructure. In this context, if AI is envisioned as an intelligence factory, then BlackRock finances everything involved in infrastructure construction, including the foundation, while Nvidia delivers only the machines.

Now, for those wanting broader exposure, BlackRock has positioned itself as a key player in data centers and energy solutions, through a major partnership with Microsoft (MSFT) and MGX (a UAE state-backed investor) in September 2024. This partnership was augmented through the participation of NVIDIA and xAI (founded by Elon Musk) in March this year and aimed to initially raise $30 billion of investments, which will potentially be expanded to $100 billion in total for AI infrastructure development.

Thus, I believe that it is BlackRock that appears to have laid the foundations of the Middle East AI infrastructure partnership. Furthermore, this endeavor got a boost by President Trump’s recent tour deals worth $3.6 trillion being signed with Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the UAE (United Arab Emirates), including a $1.4 trillion pledge by the latter to invest in the U.S. AI sector over the next decade. This includes building AI data centers, and given BlackRock’s active role in AI infrastructure funds and its collaboration with Middle Eastern investors like MGX, it can be seen as a strategic investment vehicle, given the way it is benefiting from these large-scale U.S.-Middle East deals.

I next look into how this collaboration can translate into income for the world's largest asset manager.

Fees Income Translate into high FCF margins for BlackRock

For this purpose, it works closely with major wealth funds, including Saudi Arabia’s PIF (Public Investment Fund), ADIA (Abu Dhabi Investment Authority), QIA (Qatar Investment Authority), and KIA (Kuwait Investment Authority) in the sense that these invest in BlackRock’s ETFs, index funds, and private market strategies.

Focusing on PIF to highlight BlackRock's role, it helps to manage investments and develop financial strategies, with the engagement consisting of assisting with asset allocation and risk management, covering Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030 initiatives, including green energy and technology projects. In this way, supported by BlackRock's expertise in ETFs, fixed income, and alternative assets and ability to identify opportunities, PIF has been expanding its international investments into Uber, Lucid Motors, and Meta Platforms (META). Also, for the sake of clarity, BlackRock does not directly own shares in PIF, as it is strictly an advisor. Still, the asset manager holds shares in many companies PIF invests in, notably through its various funds, creating indirect overlaps.

In this way, it collects regular investment management fees from clients subscribing to its funds (index, actively managed, and iShares ETF) based on the AUM, irrespective of whether the market goes up or down. On top, it obtains advisory fees. Moreover, one advantage is that its activities do not incur heavy physical infrastructure or manufacturing, as for the PIF investment platform, an initial $5 billion anchor was made by the Saudi government. Thus, with a capital light and highly cash generative operations consisting primarily of managing funds and providing financial software, it boasts a FCF margin of 26.6%. This is higher than the median for the financials sector by more than 40% as pictured below.

Now, one way to increase cash flow is by managing more money and making more fees. Thus, considering that it just manages 10% of the $3.6 trillion and charges 0.5%, this comes to $1.8 billion (0.2 x 3,600,000,000,000 x 0.005) in revenue.

NVIDIA Generates More Cash, but BlackRock is Better because of the Risks

By comparison, by immediately selling 18K chips at an average price of $65K, NVIDIA would obtain only $1.17 billion, which seems small considering NVIDIA’s data center sales of $30.8 billion just for the third quarter of 2025.

Now, some will argue that the $1.17 billion includes only the raw chip and not the networking, cooling, and attached software like CUDA, which means the semiconductor giant could rake in more sales thanks to value-added products. Furthermore, NVIDIA also has a capital-light model in the sense that it outsources its manufacturing to the Taiwan Semiconductor Company (TSM) while benefiting from pricing power due to huge demand for the Blackwell, which significantly exceeds the Hopper (H100) in terms of performance. This explains its relatively high FCF margins of 33.85%.

Still, there are risks that NVIDIA will sustain the initial sales momentum.

First, oil prices are relatively low, and Saudi Arabia has recorded a 43% surge in its budget deficit last year. Now, oil has maintained its downtrend, and unless commodity prices improve, the country may have to divert funds earmarked for AI infrastructure to meet budgetary needs, posing a threat to its Sovereign AI model backed by state funding.

Second, Chinese companies, like Huawei, not only operate cloud zones in the country but with its data center have a strong presence in the local developer ecosystem. To this end, given it is still unclear whether there will be restrictions on who can access the chips on site and their purpose, China hawks may delay the deal, especially since the "AI diffusion" rule places restrictions on the export of chips to most countries, including Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

Therefore, given the potentially long runway and geopolitical complexity of the $3.6 trillion investment, gaining exposure through BlackRock, as an asset allocator and infrastructure financier, makes more sense, especially given that while AI demand is real, the focus will initially be on land, buildings, and power. Furthermore, for BlackRock, the above $1.8 billion estimate is recurring money, and, on top, as investors put more money into its funds, it benefits from incremental revenue (fees) with minimal extra cost due to scalability, leading to higher FCF margins.

BlackRock deserves better after accounting for Risks

Thus, the asset manager is positioned to earn fees from each stage of the development, namely land, power, connectivity, and facility buildouts, offering an early cycle exposure, long before the chips are plugged in. Moreover, its price to free cash flow of 36.2x is much lower than Nvidia’s 51.5x, and the midpoint comes to 43.9x ((36.2+51.5)/2). Estimating a discount based on this mid-point, I have 17.4% ((43.9-36.2)/43.9), which, when applied to the share price of $993.7 at the time of writing, yields a target of $1,167 (993.7 x 1.174).

On the other hand, Moody’s downgrade of the U.S. economy may cast a shadow on certain financial firms due to higher interest rates pressuring funding costs and possibly the risk of facing tightening liquidity. Thus, a systemic financial stress could lead to a market decline, in turn triggering client de-risking and shrinking BlackRock's revenue base.

However, the asset manager does not lend or take credit risk directly like a bank (no loan book), has a highly diversified AUM (across various sectors of the economy), and generates fee-based income, not interest income. Also, its much lower debt-to-equity ratio compared to peers can mitigate the effects of a deterioration in cash flow generation in case financial conditions deteriorate.

Looking further, in addition to being diversified across various asset classes (equities, fixed income, crypto, alternatives, ETFs), it generated around 35% of its sales originating outside the U.S. in the first quarter of 2025 (Q1) as per the table below.

This should increase since it has forged strong relationships with institutions and sovereign funds from around the world, which can potentially reduce its exposure to financial tightening cycles in America. To this end, the firm sees "opportunities for improved capital markets performance outside of the United States as very much aligned with its financial growth". Moreover, the management emphasized its global presence and "helping countries grow their capital markets" in response to a question about a downturn in the U.S. economy during the Q1 earnings call.

Justifying the Bullish Case

Coming back to AI infrastructure, while the semiconductor giant only delivers the compute layer, BlackRock is diversified along the value chain with its involvement in the foundational tier (data center, fiber, real estate, energy) in addition to GPUs through its 7.3% indirect share ownership of NVIDIA in January 2024. Now, 7.3% of NVIDIA's market cap of $3.3 trillion comes to around $241 billion, while the asset manager is valued at only $153 billion, again showing that it is undervalued and justifying the bullish position.

Additionally, it holds shares of AMD (7.7% of holdings in January 2024), which secured a $10 billion deal with Humain for deployment of AI capacity over the next five years. Moreover, BlackRock, through its funds, also holds indirect stakes in Alphabet (GOOG), Qualcomm (QCOM), Cisco (CSCO), IBM (IBM), Oracle (ORCL), and Salesforce (CRM), which expect to jointly invest $80 billion in the Middle East.

Finally, this thesis has argued the case for early-stage AI infrastructure exposure with lower risk than NVIDIA. Also, a fee-based approach is more stable than a product-based one, which is also more cyclical.

data.title: Tuya: A Return To Sustainable Profitability Is Important
data.description:
Q1 FY25 earnings came in earlier today from Tuya Inc. (NYSE:TUYA), a business that is more than the typical Chinese tech company quietly chasing growth. It’s a global platform builder with real traction, and now, it’s profitable again. I think the pitch here is simple: the company is proving that it can grow revenue, improve its margins, and become sustainably profitable in AIoT (Artificial Intelligence of Things).

But, and this is a big but, Tuya also has a confluence of risk vectors. For one, it’s based in China. It sells to partners across the world, and it’s trading in a sector that re-prices optimism fairly quickly, almost on a whim. That’s why I’m going in with a cautious Buy for investors who understand the risks of going with a company with real upside but a fair bit of potential headwind.

What stands out to me now is the company’s ability to control what it can, ensuring that it’s on the right track with growth. So far, the business model has proven that it can scale globally with an ecosystem of developers, hardware partners, and cloud-based monetization.

For me, the investment case boils down to two core convictions:

  1. The platform is gaining leverage: There are more developers, more product integrations, and more recurring revenue.

  2. The business is maturing, and it’s generating the operating leverage to go with its global reach.

Q1 FY25 Performance Highlights

The results for this quarter show us something investors have wanted to see for a while a clean, upward trend in revenue and profitability.

Revenue Momentum Is Starting to Stick

Here, total revenue for the quarter was up 21% YoY, to $74.7 million, and that growth mostly came from platform expansion and increasing monetization. What’s noticeable is the consistency: this is a seasonal business, but it’s the third consecutive Q1 with high double-digit revenue growth. Clearly, the demand in this period of the fiscal year is much higher than in previous years. The platform is gaining scale in terms of meaningful usage, which is reflected in the number of developers it now has.

The key drivers are:

  • Growing developer base: Over 1.3 million developers use Tuya’s AI Cloud Developer Platform across 200 countries, so there’s much deeper integration of its tools.

  • Stronger demand in international markets: The company’s growth in Latin America and Southeast Asia is very strong. On the latter, it now has a partnership with the Singaporean government to develop a centralized energy management platform that helps them manage consumption in over 1.4 million Singapore public apartments.

Profitability Has Turned A Corner

In my view, the most meaningful headline is this: Tuya posted $11 million in net income, which is a +$14.5 million swing from the same quarter last year. It matters because while many platform companies are burning cash while chasing scale, Tuya is showing that it can do both. Also, the gross margin improved to 48.5%, compared to 47.8% in the same period in 2024, mostly helped by higher-margin SaaS offerings and better cost discipline. That’s a record in the company’s history.

Now, those aren’t massive numbers yet, but in the context of Tuya, it’s the direction that matters. The numbers are trending upwards. The company has found ways to grow while reducing costs, and the shift from topline growth to sustainable profitability is a critical thing.

The Business Is Learning to Scale Smartly

For a business that relies pretty heavily on platform volume, the scale can cut both ways: too much too fast can dilute the margins. However, I think that Tuya is scaling in stride. It’s investing in R&D, widening its partner network, and improving the unit economics of its Platform-as-a-Service (PAAS) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) models while reducing net losses to positive income.

Long story short, the financials are much improved from a year ago because the business model is working in a global context.

Other Strategic Developments

The main USP for Tuya is that it builds the infrastructure that powers smart devices globally, not just smart home software. I believe that the key to understanding the company’s long-term potential lies in its positioning. It’s not a product seller; it’s a platform provider enabling an entire ecosystem.

From Smart Devices to Smart Infrastructure

Tuya’s strategy basically is delivering Platform-as-a-Service (PAAS) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) offerings for device manufacturers, from startups to behemoths, to “plug into” intelligence. And it’s smart because it shifts the company away from that hardware dependency and towards recurring software revenues. That’s the sentiment I gathered from listening to CFO Alex Yang during the company’s quarterly earnings call. That pivot gives Tuya:

  • Better visibility into forward earnings,

  • A more defensible margin profile, and

  • A deeper relationship with its customers (OEMs and brands).

It also means that the company doesn’t carry that inventory risk or the customer acquisition costs that hardware-first companies do. So far, a lot more global electronics manufacturers are adopting Tuya’s cloud services, and that’s a momentum win. Additionally, it now supports over 3,000 product categories, from lighting and security to appliances and HVAC. From this perspective, the idea is for Tuya to power its customers, not outcompete them. That allows it to scale across verticals without experiencing brand conflict.

AI Integration, Geographic Expansion, Partnerships: Bearing Fruit Already

On the AI front, Tuya is adding large language models (LLMs) and generative AI to its operations. Think of this in terms of optimizing how the smart devices interact with themselves and humans. The AI is also improving functions like customer support and user experience.

Obviously, the company isn’t starting from scratch—it has a global base of connected devices and user data. Still, it’s an early mover here, and with that advantage, it has the raw material to make AI work, especially when it comes to energy optimization, smart security, and automated workflows. Clearly, then, AI is a performance differentiator.

Company CEO Jerry Wang also had good tidings regarding its expansion and partnerships. The Singapore one I mentioned earlier is particularly important for Tuya’s growth in Southeast Asia. Also, the company has new partnerships with Volcano engines under ByteDance, along with integrating the Doubao large language model, and it has more than a few commercial collaborations in AI wearables and home robotics.

This partnership-driven model gives Tuya the advantage of expanding quickly while avoiding many of the pitfalls that come with international scaling. Think of high sales costs and regulatory hurdles.

Valuation

Tuya’s stock doesn’t look cheap if you consider its trailing P/E ratio, but there’s more to a valuation than one ratio, especially when the company has just turned a corner in terms of profitability. Revenue is growing, margins are expanding, and there’s a lot of cash in the bank. So, I’d posit that Tuya is growing in its valuation. Here’s a snapshot of the valuation metrics as of May 21, 2025.

  • Current Share Price: ~$2.50.

  • Shares Outstanding: ~$601 million.

  • Market Cap: ~$1.55 billion.

  • TTM P/E (GAAP): 296x.

  • Forward P/E (GAAP): ~36x.

  • Price/Sales (TTM): ~5.2x (based on ~$300M trailing revenue).

  • Cash & Equivalents: ~$854 billion.

The elephant in the room is the 296x trailing P/E. That’s staggeringly high, but it’s also largely irrelevant now that Tuya is profitable. I believe that at 36x, the forward P/E ratio is still punchy, but it’s a much more reasonable multiple for a platform company in its growth phase. Let’s not forget that it also has high-margin SaaS and AI potential and expands non-GAAP net margins.

In terms of the company’s value, its enterprise value (EV) is ~$702 million, after taking out over $850 million in cash and no debt. So, its EV/Sales multiple is ~2x, which is a modest multiple for a software-led platform growing at 20%+. All things considered, the headline multiples are high, but the underlying valuation is relatively conservative when we account for cash and growth. I think that Tuya’s EV/Sales multiple is much lower than its peers in SaaS and IoT because of its China exposure and accompanying macro risks. But, and this is going off of what we’ve seen so far if the business continues to post clean, cash-positive quarters, that discount will narrow quickly.

Risks Exist, But They’re Manageable

In my view, Tuya faces some rather big risks, and I discuss them below.

  • Tariffs and trade exposure.

Tuya doesn’t ship physical goods, but its entire platform is embedded in the global smart device supply chain. As CEO Jerry Wang put it, Tuya isn’t in the trade lanes — but it’s wired into every product that is.

That’s a bit of a headache, given that in Q1 FY25, its orders from China to the US saw an immediate “pause” in late April due to new tariff talks. Sure, the U.S. only accounts for ~10% of Tuya’s revenue, but similar caution exists in parts of Southeast Asia and Mexico. This is a known risk — but the latest round of tariffs has proven more disruptive than previous cycles.

  • Geopolitical Risk.

As a Chinese tech platform, Tuya will always carry a degree of regulatory and geopolitical sensitivity. It’s especially pronounced when it comes to data privacy, U.S.-China relations, and cloud infrastructure. So, any breakdown in international tech standards (e.g., bans, nationalistic vendor requirements) may impact its future partnerships.

In my view, Tuya’s proactive strategy—expanding into Southeast Asia, opening a new Singapore office, and anchoring deployments with governments like HDB — is a clear hedge against these risks.

Final Takeaway

Tuya isn’t a speculative AIoT name that’s burning cash to scale: the numbers are good, and it’s profitable again. The company’s platform is also growing in the international space, and the AI integration is working pretty well. Everything looks to be going efficiently. So far, so good.

Having said that, the road ahead isn’t exactly free of bumps. There are macroeconomic conditions and geopolitical dynamics to navigate. So, I’m reiterating my cautious Buy recommendation, primarily for investors who are looking at the market with a high-conviction, long-term lens.

data.title: EXG: 9% Yield, International Exposure
data.description:
Looking for international high-yield exposure?

The US $ has taken a beating so far in 2025 - it's down in most major currencies, including the Euro, which is up ~9% vs. the US $.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (NYSE:EXG) has a portfolio of domestic and foreign common stocks, with an emphasis on dividend-paying stocks. Management writes call options on one or more U.S. and foreign indices to generate cash flow.

Fund Profile:

"The Fund's primary investment objective is to provide current income and gains, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation.

The Fund evaluates returns on an after-tax basis and seeks to minimize and defer federal income taxes incurred by shareholders in connection with their investment in the Fund." (EXG site).

EXG is by far the largest US closed-end fund, "CEF", in the foreign stock category, with a $2.77B asset base, and the largest average daily volume. It began in February 2007 and has 89 holdings.

It uses no leverage, but had 48% of its portfolio overwritten with call options, at an average of 3.70% out of the money, with 5 days left until expiration, as of 3/31/25

Holdings:

With 57% to North America, EXG isn't a purely international investment vehicle, but it does have ~33% exposure to Europe, and ~8% Asia/Pacific exposure.

EXG's sector exposures vs. those of the S&P 500 were overweight in Industrials, Health Care, and Consumer Discretionary; and underweight in Communication Services, Consumer Staples, Materials, and Tech, as of 3/31/25:

Like many equity CEFs, EXG's top 10 holdings included some high-flying Magnificent 7 stocks, such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), as of 3/31/25.

The top 10 positions accounted for 27.6% of the portfolio and also included European-based large stocks such as AstraZeneca, Nestlé, Siemens, and ASML.

Dividends:

EXG pays monthly distributions of $.0657. At $8.49, it yielded 9.29%. It generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays at the end of the month.

It has a modest 1% 5-year dividend growth average - its most recent hike was in April 2024, when it raised the monthly payout from $.0553 to $.0657.

EXG's fiscal year ends on 10/31. Fiscal year 2024 saw NII declining from $31M to ~$28M, but Net Realized Gains jumped from $8M to ~$118M, and unrealized appreciation more than doubled, from $221M to $452M.

Distributions shifted from ~73% Return of Capital, "ROC" in fiscal 2023, to 35% ROC in fiscal 2024, with Net Assets increasing by ~$364M, vs. $57M in fiscal 2023:

Taxes:

So far, in the fiscal year ending 10/31/2025, distributions are estimated to have come from 14.6% NII, 32% short-term gains, and 53% long-term gains, with no ROC.

Performance:

As of 4/30/25, EXG had outperformed the Morningstar Foreign CEF category on a price and NAV basis. It outperformed in 2019-2021, and 2017, and outperformed on an NAV basis in 2023.

As you'd expect with a covered call strategy, EXG has underperformed the S&P 500 and the MSCI World Index over the past 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods.

Risks:

Option-Selling Risk: As illustrated above, the selling of call options limits upside participation.

Stock Market Risk: The equities held by the fund are subject to market volatility, which could affect the fund's NAV and market price values.

Economic Risk: A recession could dampen demand for some of the fund's holdings, thereby affecting their earnings, and subsequently affecting the market prices and the fund's as well.

Foreign Currency Risk: Just as holding US $-based assets involves currency exposure, holding foreign equities involves risk from foreign currency exchange swings. So far in 2025, the falling dollar has aided certain funds with foreign exposure, but that's not always the case.

Valuations:

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day.

As of the 5/21/25 close, EXG was selling at a 6.19% discount to its daily NAV/share. That's close to its 5-year average discount of 6.48%, but not nearly as deep as its 1- and 3-year discounts of 9.46% and 8.22%, respectively.

Parting Thoughts:

As of 5/22/25, EXG was 3.75% below its 52-week high, and was overbought in its slow stochastic chart. It has bounced back 8.7% from its April low over the past month-plus.

We rate EXG a HOLD, due to its comparatively average discount to NAV. We prefer to wait for a deeper discount before buying shares.

If you're interested in other high-yield vehicles, we cover them every Friday and Sunday in our articles.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: AT&T And Lumen Technologies Strike A Win-Win Bargain
data.description:
May 21 ended up being a really interesting day for shareholders of both AT&T (NYSE:T) and Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN). This is because, after the market closed, the management teams of both firms announced a major transaction between them. In essence, telecommunications giant AT&T will be purchasing essentially all of the Mass Markets fiber business currently owned by Lumen Technologies in an all-cash transaction valued at $5.75 billion. This move seems to be beneficial to both parties.

In the case of AT&T, it will add substantially to the company's already extensive fiber assets and pave the way for more rapid growth in that space over the next few years. As for Lumen Technologies, this transaction will allow the company to substantially reduce debt and interest payments. Unfortunately, Lumen Technologies still remains in a questionable position from a fundamental perspective. So gauging what potential this business might have moving forward is more complex. But when it comes to AT&T, this is a clear win for those focused on the long haul and further justifies the "strong buy" rating that I have had on the company for quite some time now.

A Big Transaction

According to the press release issued by AT&T, it has agreed to acquire substantially all of Lumen Technologies’ Mass Market fiber business in an all-cash deal valued at $5.75 billion. Even though AT&T is a massive company, this is still a significant deal. The transaction will bring to the company around 1 million fiber subscribers spread across over 4 million fiber locations. These are customers spread across 11 different states, including seven states that the conglomerate currently does not provide fiber services in. While most of these states are fairly low populated Western and Midwestern states, it also includes Oregon and Washington.

One of the first things that might pop up in investors’ minds is what impact this will have on the company and its objectives moving forward. For instance, as you can see in the chart above, management has been working on paying down debt over the last few years. The pro forma figures for the first quarter of 2025 show net of pending transactions that it will receive capital for. For this year, there should not be any impact, since this purchase is not going to close until the first half of next year. But obviously, this will slow the company’s overall net debt reduction. And unless management decides to set aside future capital that it generates from its operations, investors can expect a near term increase in net debt once the transaction closes.

Because this purchase is not going to be completed until sometime in the first half of next year, there's currently no change to AT&T’s plans for capital spending this year. In fact, the company remains committed to repurchasing shares under the $10 billion share buyback program it previously announced, with the end goal of buying back at least $3 billion of stock by the end of this year and a similar amount next year. The company did repurchase $218 million worth of shares in the first quarter of this year. So most of the buying this year still has yet to take place. It's unlikely that this plan will impact the company's dividend either. On an annualized basis, it's currently paying about $8.36 billion back to shareholders in this manner. Nor do I expect it to change the firm's plans to allocate around $22 billion on capital projects this year.

It may seem peculiar that the company is doling out so much cash for these assets when you hear that, according to management, in the 12 to 24 months following the close of the deal, the impact is supposed to be "immaterial" to the company’s EBITDA, adjusted earnings per share, and free cash flow. But this is because management is not focused on short-term benefits. Rather, it's focused on long-term growth. You see, for years now, management has been focused heavily on growing its fiber business. The chart above illustrates the number of fiber broadband connections the company had in the most recent quarter, as well as the number it has had in all of the previous quarters dating back to the first quarter of 2021.

Obviously, this acquisition will add a sizable number of connections to these efforts. But it will also pave the way toward the company achieving an even larger number of fiber passings as time goes on. When I last wrote about the company earlier this month, I detailed how, by the end of this year, the company should meet or exceed its target of hitting 30 million fiber passings nationwide. The objective of the company was to further grow this to 50 million by 2029. Now, however, management sees this number being revised higher to 60 million by the end of 2030. That's almost double what the company has today.

The fact of the matter is that fiber offers significant growth potential to the company from a profit and cash flow perspective. In the first quarter of 2025, AT&T generated $2.1 billion in fiber revenue. That was up 23.5% compared to the $1.7 billion reported the previous year. For all of 2024, fiber revenue amounted to $7.4 billion. And management continues to have comfortably above 261,000 net additions to its network each quarter. The beautiful thing about fiber, it is also bringing higher revenue than the alternative.

During the first quarter of 2025, the firm generated $72.85 per month in ARPU (average revenue per user) from its fiber operations. That represented an increase of 6.2% compared to the $68.61 reported the previous year. This has helped it to grow its EBITDA coming from fiber to $1.3 billion on a quarterly basis. That's up from the $1.1 billion reported at the same time last year. Some of the growth potential comes from the fact that the company sees fiber as a great way to essentially bundle services that it offers to customers. This includes not only for residential customers, but for business customers as well. It can also support the company’s 5G mobile network backhaul. As an example, the company found during the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year that 3.9 million of its fiber subscribers also had mobility subscriptions with the company. That's about 40.2% of all fiber subscribers. That's up from the 3.4 million, or 39.2%, that the company achieved one year earlier. This not only brings in additional revenue. It also creates product stickiness.

Lumen Benefits From This

There is no denying, at least in my opinion, that this will be a long-term benefit to AT&T and its investors. Of course, we will have to wait quite a while to see the full impact this will have on revenue, profits, and cash flows. As for Lumen Technologies, the benefit will be immediate and significant. But before I get into that, I should touch on what Lumen Technologies is and what it does. After all, with a market capitalization as of this writing of $3.91 billion, it's substantially smaller than AT&T. So most investors might not be aware of its existence.

The management team at Lumen Technologies describes the company as a networking firm that's focused on connecting people, data, and applications, securely, quickly, and effortlessly. The firm has four different brands under its belt. The first of these, Lumen, is its flagship brand that includes its Private Connectivity Fabric network architecture, Lumen Digital products, and its priority services such as Edge, Network-as-a-Service, and cybersecurity. This particular part of the company services enterprise and wholesale markets. Then, we have Quantum Fiber, which provides fiber-based broadband services to both residential and small business customers. CenturyLink provides mostly mass marketed copper-based communications services that are, frankly, on the way out long term. And finally, there is Black Lotus Labs, which serves as the company's cyber threat research and intelligence arm.

At the end of its 2024 fiscal year, Lumen Technologies boasted 163,000 fiber on-net buildings and 340,000 route miles of fiber optic cable spread across the markets in which it operates. This includes not only North America, but also part of the Asia Pacific region. Unfortunately, the last few years have not been particularly pleasant for the company. From 2022 through 2024, for instance, revenue dropped from $17.48 billion to $13.11 billion. This was driven by a decline in its Business segment from $13.10 billion to $10.36 billion, and a decline in its Mass Markets segment from $4.38 billion to $2.75 billion.

Some of this drop has been intentional. For instance, in 2023, the company sold off its EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) operations and some of its content delivery network contracts. That caused a roughly $547 million decrease in revenue from 2023 to 2024. And the sale of its Latin America and ILEC businesses the year before that, as well as the sale of its EMEA business, caused revenue to drop by $2.1 billion from 2022 to 2023.

Clearly, this business is no stranger to selling off assets. However, this also has a deleterious effect on profitability and cash flow. As the first chart in this section illustrates, net income has been all over the map. But more consistent has been some of its cash flow data. From 2022 to 2023, operating cash flow plunged from $4.74 billion to $2.16 billion. There was a jump to $4.33 billion last year. We have seen a similar trend when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow, which strips out changes in working capital. But unfortunately, EBITDA has been on a consistent decline, dropping from $6.86 billion to $3.94 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics have all been declining during this window of time. When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management has said that investors should anticipate EBITDA of between $3.2 billion and $3.4 billion. Operating cash flow, meanwhile, should be somewhere between $4.8 billion and $5.2 billion. The good news here is that operating cash flow forecasts are up compared to what the company saw last year. But I don't like seeing the decline in EBITDA.

The reason why management is engaging in this transaction is because the company has a bit of a problem with debt. At the end of the most recent quarter, it had net debt of $15.76 billion. That's an astronomical amount of net debt for a company with a market capitalization of $3.91 billion. This asset sale will allow the company to achieve about $4.8 billion in debt reduction. It should also boost cash flow, both in the near term and in the long run. You see, according to management, it will reduce the company's capital expenditure requirements by $1 billion. The reduction in debt should also lower annual interest expense by roughly $300 million. Based on my own estimates, actual EBITDA for the company should be lower this year by around $333 million if we were to assume that this transaction occurred at the start of this year. But operating cash flow would likely be down by only $33 million.

This is a small price to pay for a substantial reduction in leverage. You see, without the deal taking place, management estimated that the net leverage ratio for the business would be 4.9 by the end of this year. That compares to the 4.0 we get using data from the end of 2024. But adjusted for this sale, the net leverage ratio for the business should be closer to 3.9. On top of reducing overall debt by $4.8 billion, this transaction will reduce the 2029 and 2030 maturities that the company has coming due by 40%. This, and the improvement in cash flows, should in theory give the business some additional breathing room.

It also, in management's eyes, will help the company achieve its goal of focusing more on its enterprise operations. By retaining its core fiber infrastructure that focuses on enterprise customers, the company still has an opportunity to achieve growth in the long run. As evidenced by the large portion of revenue that comes from its Business segment compared to the rest of the firm, it's clear that this is just another move to further its ambitions in this regard. In fact, management hopes to achieve 47 million intercity fiber miles by the year 2028. This is up from 16.6 million that the company said that it would have by the end of this year.

There seems to be tremendous opportunity here for growth, particularly with the rise of AI and the demand it could have on infrastructure. On top of growth and this reduction in leverage that this transaction will bring to the table, it's also worth pointing out that management has been making some other changes as well. For instance, when it announced financial results for the first quarter of this year in early May, the company stated that it's making major changes to parts of its business, including unifying its network architecture and leveraging AI for automation purposes. The end goal here is to achieve annual run rate cost savings of $250 million by the end of 2025. And by 2027, it expects savings to grow to $1 billion. The company also continues to focus on other opportunities, such as its collaboration with Google Cloud to offer direct fiber access with speeds of up to 400 gigabytes per second. Management's claim is that this opens up a new $15 billion total addressable market opportunity for shareholders. All told, management believes that the enterprise market in the space in which it operates is worth around $80 billion.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that the impact on the company's bottom line from this purchase alone should be a slight reduction in both operating cash flow and EBITDA. Admittedly, this is just based on taking the estimated revenue from the sale of these assets and stripping out a proportional amount of EBITDA. Because management has not provided any concrete numbers themselves, the actual end result could be different. Having said that, the one downside that I see from this is that it will reduce one of the few areas of growth that the company has seen in recent years.

From 2022 to 2024, fiber broadband revenue under the Mass Markets segment for the company grew from $604 million to $736 million. This was in spite of some declines caused by other changes that the company made, such as a $41 million decrease from 2022 to 2023 caused by the firm's sale of its ILEC business. So actual organic revenue would have grown even faster, with management attributing this to an increase in the number of fiber customers and the continued increase in enabled locations for the firm’s Quantum Fiber buildout. For the first quarter of this year, fiber broadband revenue hit $209 million. That's up from the $170 million you reported the previous year.

Now, it's worth mentioning that not all of this revenue will disappear because of this transaction. Keep in mind that management said that the deal includes "substantially all" of the assets included in these operations. However, according to management, over the trailing four quarters ending in the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, total revenue that's being sold off will amount to $750 million. But according to my calculation, the fiber broadband operations of the company as a whole brought in $775 million during this window of time. So even though it won't be material, the company will be retaining some of the revenue from these operations. Regardless, it's bittersweet to see such a rapidly growing piece of the pie be sold off. But if it means that the company can reduce debt, reduce capital spending to boost free cash flows, and focus on its larger market, then the deal is probably worth it.

As for the value of the company, in the chart above, you can see how shares of the business are priced on both a price to operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis using estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. This chart also shows my estimate of the value for the company based on the pro forma figures that strip out this additional net debt and the estimated changes in cash flows as a result of the deal. Notably, on an EV to EBITDA basis, Lumen Technologies is getting cheaper because of this transaction.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I'm a fan of this transaction. In the long run, this should bode well for AT&T and its shareholders. It will further the company's growth initiatives, specifically in areas where it's most focused and has the greatest potential. The only downside is that it will increase leverage compared to not making the purchase and won't have a material impact on revenue, profits, or cash flows, in the near term. But as a long-term investor, I don't mind that. As for Lumen Technologies, the transaction is more complicated. The good news is that the net benefit for the company should be positive because of the debt reduction and the reduction in capital expenditure requirements moving forward. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining a "strong buy" rating on AT&T is appropriate. And as for Lumen Technologies, I believe that a "buy" rating makes sense.

data.title: FBTC: Breaking Out On Fiat Fears
data.description:
The Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund ETF (BATS:FBTC) broke to new all-time highs this week, exceeding the $94.8 peak set in December of last year. This article explores the drivers behind the break-out, and why it may not be a good idea to chase the rally at these levels.

A Quick Look at FBTC

FBTC was launched in January 2024 and has become one of the most popular Bitcoin ETFs. Its low expense ratio of 0.25% and excellent liquidity are an attraction, as is the well-known and trusted name behind the product, "Fidelity." To be completely honest, there is very little to recommend it above its main competitor, the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), which has a slightly better performance (only 0.13% since inception and probably from a tracking error), and higher AUM.

However, if you have a large exposure to Bitcoin, I would consider FBTC as an alternative to IBIT and spread the risk between the two funds. One consideration is that they have different custodians and Fidelity is self-custodian.

Driving the Break-out

FBTC often has a high-beta correlation to other risk assets such as stocks and the weakness in the S&P500 (SPY) in Q1 was replicated in FBTC. Both bottomed on the exact same day, April 7th.

There are periods where FBTC will out or under-perform due to various reasons. This was evident following the election when the new administration's crypto-friendly stance sparked a large rally. It was also the case this week as FBTC and SPY have moved in opposite directions and FBTC broke to a new all-time high.

The driver this week appears to be Moody's downgrade of US government debt. This sparked a rise in yields as the risk of buying/holding US Treasuries has increased. It also dropped the US dollar, and it was notable that when stocks and bonds recovered on Monday 19th May (the first trading session after the downgrade was announced), the US dollar stayed weak. After a feeble recovery in May, it looks to be heading for further lows.

Here is some of the rationale for the downgrade:

Without adjustments to taxation and spending, we expect budget flexibility to remain limited, with mandatory spending, including interest expense, projected to rise to around 78% of total spending by 2035 from about 73% in 2024. If the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act is extended, which is our base case, it will add around $4 trillion to the federal fiscal primary (excluding interest payments) deficit over the next decade.

As a result, we expect federal deficits to widen, reaching nearly 9% of GDP by 2035, up from 6.4% in 2024, driven mainly by increased interest payments on debt, rising entitlement spending, and relatively low revenue generation. We anticipate that the federal debt burden will rise to about 134% of GDP by 2035, compared to 98% in 2024.

And here's a shocking projection:

Federal interest payments are likely to absorb around 30% of revenue by 2035, up from about 18% in 2024 and 9% in 2021.

None of this is particularly new - Fitch, and Standard and Poor's already downgraded the credit rating years ago, and it was a matter of time before Moody's followed suit. But this puts some worrying calculations back in the spotlight. In the long term, the debt pile will erode the value of the dollar and all other fiat currencies. Crypto (and FBTC) is attractive as it has no sovereign risk, no debt, and no dilution. This is why it diverged from other risk assets this week and broke to a new all-time high.

A Temporary Tailwind

Breaking to new highs when stocks are moving in the other direction is impressive, but we don't want to see the two asset classes diverge for too long as they tend to rally and correct together over the longer term.

A sustained period of FBTC outperformance would likely need a significant and new driver. A strategic Bitcoin reserve would fit the bill, but that now seems a dead-end given the project has to be budget-neutral.

As already outlined, the reasons for the divergence this week are not new developments - crypto's main attraction has always been its independence from what many see as a doomed system of fiat currencies. Moody's downgrade may have brought some issues to the fore again, but nothing specific sparked the move.

In short, I do not expect the divergence to last very long, and since stocks look to be rolling over - or at least unlikely to break to new highs any time soon - the break-out in FBTC may also stall. As I am writing this, I see both stocks and bitcoin have dropped as President Trump has threatened the EU with 50% tariffs. This isn't necessarily bearish, but we may see a consolidation like we did the last time Bitcoin approached a previous all-time high. The current pattern and the larger-scale pattern from 2022-2025 are quite similar.

Implications

The main takeaway is to not get carried away with this break-out. The outlook for FBTC is bullish, but I would not rush in to buy at new all-time highs. A pullback to the gap around $85 would be a more compelling buying opportunity over the summer.

For those holding long-term, this week's developments underpin why crypto is a good investment, but don't really change anything.

Conclusions

FBTC and stocks diverged this week following the Moody's downgrade. While the rally was welcome for bitcoin bulls, the drivers do not look like igniting a sustained divergence as the downgrade was expected and not due to anything particularly new.

Without a "risk on" environment that also lifts stocks, the break-out may not be sustained and a drift-down/consolidation looks likely. FBTC is long-term bullish, and I rate it a "hold," but I would wait to initiate new positions nearer $85.

data.title: Did You Think It Was Over? New Tariffs Are Coming (And Maybe Another Crash)
data.description:
Apple and Europe Under Scrutiny

After a disastrous beginning in April, the suspension of tariffs and the de-escalation with China led me to believe (perhaps mistakenly) that the situation was stabilizing. Let's be clear, tariffs have not been eliminated even after the suspension; currently, there is a universal tariff of 10% on all imports and 30% on imports from China. However, compared to 145%, significant improvements had been made which spread enthusiasm across the markets.

The tariff issue was moving towards a resolution, and the only concern remaining was whether the tariffs currently in place were still too high to generate high inflation. We will only know for sure in a few months' time, but today's events have left me rather perplexed.

President Trump has announced that if Apple Inc. (AAPL) does not manufacture its iPhones sold in the US (> 60 million per year) domestically, it will face a tariff of at least 25%. This statement comes after several weeks of meetings between President Trump and Apple CEO Tim Cook, during which no agreement was reached between the two parties. Due to US-China tensions, Apple wants to gradually move production of its iPhones sold in the US to India (where it already has multiple factories), but this does not seem to particularly please President Trump. One of his main goals is to revive American manufacturing, and what better way to do so than to have one of the best American companies in history manufacture its products domestically? The point is that Apple clearly does not manufacture its iPhones in the US because of the high cost of labor compared to China and India. It is not a question of wanting to, but of being unable to.

At the time of writing, the stock is down about 3%, but as far as I am concerned, this is perhaps too little given the seriousness of the situation. Apple does not seem willing to move its production to the US, as there is currently no supply chain to produce an iPhone and huge investments would be required. We cannot know exactly how much an iPhone produced in America would cost; certainly much more than the current price, and demand would take a hit.

It is a very complicated situation because this ‘conflict’ is uneven. Unlike China, which has the leverage to stand up to the US, Apple is a company and cannot win against the country where it is based. If no agreement is reached between Apple and the President, the situation could escalate, given that he referred to a tariff of at least 25%. Therefore, if the situation escalates, it could reach higher levels and sink Apple. I don't know how far it will go, but what is certain is that this decision highlights a rather worrying aspect that does not only concern Apple.

If one of the best companies in the country can be targeted and sunk because it does not manufacture its products internally, no company is truly safe.

President Trump recently announced his disappointment with Walmart after the company said it would raise prices due to tariffs. Should shareholders be worried at this point?

Mattel fared even worse when CEO Ynon Kreiz announced that they were considering producing fewer toys in China and moving to other countries, but not the US.

That’s OK, let him go, and we’ll put a 100% tariff on his toys, and he won’t sell one toy in the United States, and that’s their biggest market.

President Trump about production shift not in the U.S.

In an economic environment where companies can be targeted and forced to do what they don't want to do, there is a risk of capital flight. Investors want clarity, and a lack of trust discourages them from buying companies in that country. A similar situation occurred a few years ago in China, where the government intervened directly in the expansion plans of the country's top companies. Ant Group's IPO was canceled, Alibaba and Tencent were fined and forced to review their internal corporate policies. In addition, a series of rules were issued for the common good, but these did not represent the good of those companies. For Apple, manufacturing in the US is not the best economic choice, but it may be forced to do so.

An ad hoc sanction against Apple seems to be the beginning of a context in which companies can operate freely, but only up to a certain limit. This limit is represented by President Trump's will, but this type of situation does not please the financial markets. In fact, futures on the major stock indices are in sharp decline.

In addition to the Apple issue, a few minutes later, there was also an announcement for Europe.

If an agreement is not reached quickly, Europe risks facing tariffs of 50% starting June 1; these would even be higher than those imposed on China. Given the political fragmentation of European countries, I find it difficult to see how an agreement can be reached in such a short time.

This situation, combined with all the others, is creating serious doubts among investors, and the market does not like uncertainty.

In recent weeks, we have been approaching all-time highs, but this news leads me to believe that the tariff issue will continue for some time. It is not just a matter of ‘Liberation Day’, it is something much bigger and more complex. Individual companies that do not follow the President's will and countries that are slow to negotiate are now under pressure. Who will be the next target?
